subject: ap-comparative-government
theme: Political systems differ fundamentally in how they distribute power, derive legitimacy, and manage the relationship between state authority and individual freedom — making regime type the lens through which all six course countries are compared.

core_concepts.title: State, Nation, Regime, Government
core_concepts.detail: These four terms are distinct and frequently confused on exams. A state is a political organization with a permanent population, defined territory, governing institutions, and international recognition. A nation is a cultural or ethnic community sharing language, religion, or identity — a nation need not have its own state (e.g., the Kurdish people). A regime is the set of fundamental rules governing who holds power and how it is exercised; regimes endure across many governments. A government is the specific set of officeholders at a given moment — it changes with elections or coups. One regime can have many governments. Sovereignty is the state's supreme legal authority over its territory and population, free from external control.

core_concepts.title: Democratic vs. Authoritarian Regimes
core_concepts.detail: A regime is classified on a spectrum from liberal democracy to authoritarianism based on five key indicators: (1) adherence to rule of law (the principle that all persons and officials are equally bound by law), (2) media freedom, (3) free and fair elections, (4) governmental transparency, and (5) citizen participation. The UK is the course anchor for liberal democracy — rule of law, independent judiciary, free elections, devolution. Russia is classified as a hybrid or illiberal democracy — it holds elections but manipulates them to guarantee outcomes, controls media, and restricts civil liberties. China is a one-party authoritarian state operating by rule by law (law as a tool of state control). Iran is a theocratic authoritarian regime under velayat-e faqih (clerical rule). Mexico and Nigeria are transitional democracies — genuinely competitive but not fully consolidated.

core_concepts.title: Federal vs. Unitary Systems
core_concepts.detail: Federal systems divide power between a national government and regional governments, each with constitutionally defined authority. Mexico, Nigeria, and Russia are the CED's federal examples. Unitary systems concentrate power at the national level; subnational bodies exist at the central government's discretion. China, Iran, and the UK are unitary. A critical nuance: the UK has practiced devolution — transferring specific legislative powers to the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, and Northern Ireland Assembly — but remains formally unitary because Westminster retains parliamentary sovereignty and could theoretically reverse devolution. Students commonly misclassify the UK as federal because of devolution.

core_concepts.title: Sources of Legitimacy and Authority
core_concepts.detail: Legitimacy is the widely held belief that the government has the right to rule. Sources include: rational-legal authority (constitutions, formal laws — UK, Mexico, Nigeria), traditional authority (British monarchy), charismatic authority (a leader's personal appeal), religious authority (Iran's velayat-e faqih doctrine bases Supreme Leader authority on Shia Islamic jurisprudence), nationalist ideology (China's CPC ties legitimacy to economic growth and national revival), and political party endorsement (both China and Russia rely on ruling-party approval as a legitimacy source). Legitimacy can be undermined by corruption, electoral fraud disputes, and poor economic performance — and reinforced by policy effectiveness, peaceful power transfer, and reduced corruption.

core_concepts.title: Democratization and Democratic Consolidation
core_concepts.detail: Democratization is the transition from authoritarian to democratic rule, typically involving freer elections, expanded civil liberties, rule of law, and universal suffrage. Mexico's transition (from PRI one-party dominance ending with Vicente Fox's 2000 PAN victory) and Nigeria's return to civilian rule in 1999 are the CED's primary democratization examples. Democratic consolidation is reached when democratic institutions are so embedded that reversion to authoritarianism is extremely unlikely without an external shock — the UK exemplifies consolidation; Mexico and Nigeria are still consolidating. Russia illustrates democratic backsliding: the post-Soviet opening was reversed as election rules tightened and civil liberties eroded.

core_concepts.title: Internal Actors and Regime Stability
core_concepts.detail: Internal actors — protest movements, civil society, separatists, armed groups — can either bolster or undermine regime stability. Key CED examples: Iran's Green Movement (2009, mass protests after disputed election results — undermined legitimacy), Mexico's Zapatista uprising (1994, indigenous rebellion in Chiapas challenging state authority), Nigeria's MEND and MOSOP (militants and advocates demanding Niger Delta oil revenue redistribution), Nigeria's Boko Haram (Islamist insurgency threatening state stability), and Russia's Chechen separatism (armed conflict challenging central authority). States respond with suppression, negotiation, or institutional reform. The creation of INE (Mexico's independent electoral institute) is an example of institutional reform driven by internal pressure for transparency.

core_concepts.title: Methods of Comparative Political Analysis
core_concepts.detail: Political scientists use quantitative data (GDP per capita, Freedom House ratings, Gini coefficient, HDI) and qualitative data (speeches, constitutional documents, political cartoons) to compare countries. Key distinction: correlation means two variables are statistically associated; causation requires a demonstrated mechanism — causation is very difficult to establish in comparative politics due to confounding variables. Another critical distinction: an empirical statement is an objective, testable claim; a normative statement is a value judgment about what ought to be. The HDI measures human development (income + education + life expectancy). The Gini coefficient measures income inequality (0 = perfect equality; higher = more unequal). Freedom House rates countries as Free, Partly Free, or Not Free.

exam_tip: The AP exam consistently tests three high-frequency distinctions from Unit 1: (1) regime vs. government — a regime sets the rules for power, a government is the specific officeholders; (2) rule of law (state bound by law, UK model) vs. rule by law (state uses law as a tool, China model); (3) federal vs. unitary systems — memorize that Mexico, Nigeria, and Russia are federal while China, Iran, and the UK are unitary, and be prepared to explain why the UK's devolution does not make it federal.
